text.content:
Launching a Rest

Only the GM can start a rest. Two ways to do it:

  • Chat command - type /respite in the chat bar.
  • Scene control - click the campground icon in the token controls toolbar (left side of the canvas).

The module intercepts long rests by default. When a player clicks "Long Rest" on their character sheet, Respite opens instead of the standard dialog. This can be toggled off in settings.

The Four Phases

Each rest runs through four phases in order.

1. Setup

The GM picks the terrain, shelter type (safe, sheltered, rough, or exposed), and weather conditions. These determine the baseline encounter DC - how likely the party is to be interrupted. A visible breakdown shows how each factor (fire, scouting, defenses) shifts the odds.

2. Activities

Each character picks one camp activity for the night. GMs assign activities for NPCs, and players pick their own.

  • Guard - Lowers the encounter DC. Armor-dependent bonuses if wearing medium/heavy.
  • Scout - Perception check to lower the encounter threshold. Can detect threats early.
  • Rest Fully - No encounter benefit, but the character recovers without penalties.
  • Fortify - Raises the camp defenses. Stacks with other fortifiers.
  • Train - No camp benefit. Purely a narrative option.
  • Campfire - Tends the fire. Terrain-specific difficulty. Magical shelter and taverns skip this.
  • Study / Identify - Work with magical items, copy spells (Wizards), or run a Detect Magic scan.

3. Events

The GM rolls against the encounter DC. Events are pulled from terrain-tagged pools with weighted selection. Some events are positive (a merchant caravan passes), some negative (a storm rolls in), some are decision trees where the GM picks how the party responds. The system tracks event history so the same disaster does not repeat until the pool cycles.

4. Resolution

HP, hit dice, spell slots, and exhaustion are processed according to the comfort level. Each player gets a private whisper with their personal recovery summary. The GM sees the full breakdown.

Terrain

Terrain sets the event pool and base encounter difficulty. Available terrains include forest, desert, swamp, urban, dungeon, tavern, and more. Each terrain has its own set of events and baseline DC.

Comfort Levels

Comfort determines how much the party recovers. The GM sets this during setup or uses the module default.

  • Safe - Full HP, full HD, full spell slots. Exhaustion reduced by 1.
  • Sheltered - Full HP, half HD rounded up. Full spell slots. Exhaustion reduced by 1.
  • Rough - Half HP rounded up, half HD rounded up. Full spell slots. Exhaustion reduced by 1.
  • Exposed - Half HP rounded up, quarter HD (minimum 1). Full spell slots. No exhaustion reduction.

Food and Water Tracking

When meal tracking is enabled in settings, a rations phase appears between Activities and Events. Each character needs food and water for the rest period.

Players drag ration items from their inventory onto the food and water plates. If a character goes without food or water, they may need to make Constitution saves or gain exhaustion, following the standard 5e variant rules.

Multi-day rests (when the party has been traveling for several days since their last rest) require provisions for each day.

Meal tracking is optional and can be toggled off entirely in module settings.

Module Settings

All settings are under Game Settings > Module Settings > Ionrift Respite.

  • Default Comfort Level - The comfort tier used when the GM does not pick one during setup.
  • Intercept Player Rests - When enabled, clicking "Long Rest" on a character sheet opens the Respite flow instead of the default dialog.
  • Armor Doff/Don Advisory - Shows a reminder when characters are resting in heavy armor (Xanathar's rules).
  • Study Activity - Enables the Study activity tile for identifying items, copying spells, and Detect Magic scans.
  • Track Food & Water - Enables the rations phase. When off, the rest skips straight from Activities to Events.
  • Partial Sustenance - Allows partial food/water (half rations) without full penalties.
  • Content Packs - Enable or disable expansion event packs. Shows event counts per terrain.

Event Coverage

Respite ships with 31 hand-written events across 6 terrains. Each event has up to 4 outcome tiers (triumph, success, mixed, failure), producing over 120 distinct narrative outcomes. Events are weighted, terrain-tagged, and never repeat until the pool cycles.

Forest (5 events)

  • Pack Circling (encounter) - Wolves test the firelight perimeter.
  • Widowmaker (complication) - A dead branch shifts in the canopy above a sleeper.
  • Creeping Fog (complication) - Thick fog rolls in from the lowland.
  • Moonlit Clearing (discovery) - The watch finds a clearing with edible mushrooms and herbs.
  • Dawn Chorus (ambient) - Birdsong at first light. A good omen.

Desert (5 events)

  • Sand Burial (complication) - Shifting dunes threaten to swallow gear.
  • Scorpion Nest (encounter) - Something crawls out of the sand.
  • Water Shortage (complication) - The dry air takes its toll on supplies.
  • Buried Trade Goods (discovery) - Half-buried cargo from a lost caravan.
  • Desert Sky (ambient) - Stars so dense they cast shadows.

Dungeon (5 events)

  • Wandering Patrol (encounter) - Footsteps echo from a nearby corridor.
  • Vermin Nest (complication) - Rats or insects disturbed by the camp.
  • Stale Air (complication) - The air thickens and breathing gets harder.
  • Previous Expedition (discovery) - Remains of an older adventuring party.
  • Claw Marks (ambient) - Deep scratches scored into the stone walls.

Swamp (5 events)

  • Swamp Stalkers (encounter) - Something tracks the party from the waterline.
  • Leech Infestation (complication) - Leeches find exposed skin overnight.
  • Foul Miasma (complication) - Toxic gas rises from the bog.
  • Bog Rot (complication) - Wounds fester in the damp air.
  • Dancing Lights (ambient) - Strange lights drift across the marsh surface.

Urban (5 events)

  • Light Fingers (encounter) - A thief tests the party's awareness.
  • Street Disturbance (complication) - Noise from the street disrupts rest.
  • Late-Night Peddler (discovery) - A merchant with unusual wares.
  • City Watch Patrol (complication) - Guards check the area.
  • City Sounds (ambient) - The city never fully sleeps.

Tavern (5 events)

  • A Familiar Tune (ambient) - A bard plays something the party recognises.
  • Local Gossip (ambient) - Overheard conversation at the next table.
  • Tense Words (ambient) - An argument brewing between locals.
  • Wandering Merchant (ambient) - A trader offers goods to travellers.
  • The Local Cat (ambient) - A tavern cat investigates the newcomers.

Disasters (1 event)

  • Flash Flood (complication) - A wall of water surges through the campsite. Triggered on natural 1.

Event Categories

CategoryWhat it means
EncounterSomething hostile or confrontational. May lead to combat.
ComplicationA problem that needs solving. Skill checks, resource loss, condition risks.
DiscoverySomething useful found. Items, information, or resources.
AmbientColour and atmosphere. No mechanical impact, but sets the tone.

Outcome Tiers

Most events resolve through a skill check with four possible outcomes:

  • Triumph - Beat the DC by 5 or more. Best possible result.
  • Success - Meet the DC. Problem handled cleanly.
  • Mixed - Fail by less than 5. Partial success with a cost.
  • Failure - Miss the DC by 5 or more. Full consequences.

The GM can Force Pass or Force Fail any check from the event card UI.
